segments.text: Part 6G: electron transport chain Dr. Rizk

Electron transport chain
Complex I, II, III and IV: proteins imbedded in the membrane, transport electrons
Other components: Coenzyme Q and cytochrome c
Oxygen is the final acceptor of electrons at Complex IV
As electrons are transported, energy is used to pump protons to intermembrane space
Complex I, III and IV (not complex II) pump protons
Proton gradient is used to generate ATP using ATP synthase

Electron Transport Chain Electrons in motion
Complex I → Co Q → Complex III → cyt c → Complex IV → O 2
↑                ↑
NADH Complex II (From TCA cycle)
Complex I: NADH dehydrogenase contains FMN
CoQ : coenzyme Q, an electron carrier from I and II to III
Complex II: succinate dehydrogenase (TCA cycle)
Complex III: donates electrons to cyt c
Cytochrome c : protein contains heme , gives electrons to complex IV
Complex IV: donates electrons to Oxygen to make Water
[Speaker Notes] Correct diagram in book

Complex I Oxidizes NADH and Reduces Coenzyme Q
NADH- CoQ Reductase,or NADH dehydrogenase
Complex I carries out electron transfer from NADH to Coenzyme Q (redox reaction)
Electrons move from NADH to CoQ , protons are pumped from the matrix to intermembrane space

Electron Transport Chain
Coenzyme Q is a mobile electron carrier imbedded in the membrane
Shuttles electrons from Complexes I and II to III
Long hydrophobic tail: membrane anchor
CoQ accepts electrons from Complex I and complex II
Passes electrons on to Complex III

Electron transport chain
Ubiquinone is anchored to the membrane near Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase)
Succinate to Fumarate reaction produces FADH2
FADH2 is reoxidized to FAD by giving electrons to Coenzyme Q
No protons are pumped in the process

Complex II Oxidizes Succinate and Reduces Coenzyme Q
Complex II contains Iron sulfur clusters and a Heme -bound Iron, all mediate electron transport
Fe 3+ is reduced to Fe 2+ , then recycled by giving e - to CoQ
This arrow is wrong!

Complex II Oxidizes Succinate and Reduces Coenzyme Q
Figure 20.6 (b) The structure of Complex II from pig heart.
(c) The arrangement of redox centers.  Electron flow is from bottom to top.

Electron transport chain
Complex III accepts electrons from CoQ , passes them along to cytochrome c
Complex III is also called CoQ -cytochrome c reductase
Protons are pumped in the process

Electron Transport Chain
Cytochrome c is a small soluble heme protein
Cytochrome group in cyt c protein is a heme that coordinates iron, which facilitates electron transport between Complex III and Complex IV

Electron transport chain
Complex IV:
accepts electrons from cyt c
is also known as cyt c oxidase
gives electrons to oxygen (water is produced) on the matrix side
Additional protons are pumped

Complex IV Transfers Electrons from Cytochrome c to Reduce Oxygen on the Matrix Side
Figure 20.14 The complete structure of bovine cytochrome c oxidase.
As in Figure 20.13, subunit I is purple, subunit II is yellow, and subunit III is blue.

Complex IV Transfers Electrons from Cytochrome c to Reduce Oxygen on the Matrix Side
Figure 20.15  The electron-transfer pathway for cytochrome c oxidase. Cytochrome c binds on the cytosolic face, transferring electrons through the copper and heme centers to reduce O 2 on the matrix side of the membrane.

Figure 20.17 The proton channels of cytochrome c oxidase from R. spheroides
Protons are presumed to “ hop ” along arrays of water molecules.

A model for the electron-transport pathway in the mitochondrial inner membrane.
Figure 20.18 (b)  A model for the electron-transport pathway in the mitochondrial inner membrane.  UQ/UQH2 and cyt c are mobile carriers and transfer electrons between the complexes.

Making Energy
Complexes I, III and IV (not complex II) pump protons from matrix to inter-membrane space of mitochondria
F1-Fo ATP synthase uses proton gradient to make ATP from ADP and Phosphate
(oxidative phosphorylation)
H +
H +
ADP
ATP
[Speaker Notes] Make fig

Electron Transfer Energy Stored in a Proton Gradient: The Mitchell Hypothesis
The coupling between oxidation and phosphorylation was a mystery for many years
Many biochemists squandered careers searching for the elusive "high energy intermediate"
Peter Mitchell proposed a novel idea - a proton gradient across the inner membrane could be used to drive ATP synthesis
The proton gradient is created by the proteins of the electron-transport pathway
Mitchell was ridiculed, but the chemiosmotic hypothesis eventually won him a Nobel prize

Electron Transfer Energy Stored in a Proton Gradient: The Mitchell Hypothesis
Figure 20.19 The proton and electrochemical gradients existing across the inner mitochondrial membrane.

ATP Synthase is Composed of F 1 and F 0
Figure 20.20 The ATP synthase, a rotating molecular motor.  The c , γ and ε subunits constitute the rotating portion (the rotor) of the motor. The b, d and h subunits form a long, slender stalk that connects F 0 in the membrane and F 1 . Flow of protons from the a -subunit through the c -subunits turns the rotor and drives the cycle of conformation changes in the α - and β -subunits of F 1 that synthesize ATP.

The Catalytic Sites of ATP Synthase Adopt Three Different Conformations
Alternating α and β subunits with 1 γ subunit (purple) extends through the center of the hexamer .
As the γ subunit rotates, each α / β dimer adopts a different confomration
Each conformation is a different step in ATP fromation
Cool animation: Here [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=b_cp8MsnZFA]

Uncoupling agents
Agents that allow protons to cross the inner mitochondrial membrane without going through ATP synthase leading to a rapid consumption of energy without generation of ATP
H +
H +
ADP
ATP
[Speaker Notes] Make Fig

Electron Transport Chain Uncoupling agents
Thermogenin – natural uncoupling protein 1 (UCP1) found in the mitochondria of brown adipose tissue. It is used to generate heat by non-shivering thermogenesis to keep newborns internal temperature higher
UCP1 provides an alternative way for protons to enter into mitochondrial matrix and bypass the ATP synthase → ↑heat production in the absence of ATP synthesis

Electron Transport Chain Inhibitors
Cyanide, Carbon Monoxide and Azide
bind  to cytochrome c oxidase  → prevention of transport of electron to O 2 ( Complex IV )
blockage of ATP production, low O 2 consumption
Complex I → Co Q → Complex III → cyt c → Complex IV → O 2
↑                ↑
NADH Complex II (From TCA cycle)

Electron Transport Chain Inhibitors
Cyanide, Carbon Monoxide and Azide
Complex IV contains Fe, which coordinates oxygen during the electron transport process to produce water
Fe in Hemoglobin is also blocked by CN, CO and N 3
Complex I → Co Q → Complex III → cyt c → Complex IV → O 2
↑                ↑
NADH Complex II (From TCA cycle)
